source.title: How do you feel -- now? The anterior insula and human awareness

confidence_rationale: Canonical review establishing AIC as the re-representation locus of interoceptive state, with direct autonomic and affective efferents. Provides the biological grounding for SD-032c's claim that AIC integrates z_harm_a with interoceptive baseline (drive_level). Mapping fidelity is moderate: Craig's framework focuses on awareness and subjective feeling, not on the urgency-interrupt / mode-switch function specifically; that extension requires Menon & Uddin 2010.
failure_signatures: If AIC activity is primarily a readout of body state (awareness) rather than a trigger for mode changes, SD-032c's urgency-interrupt role is ungrounded.
If interoceptive salience in AIC does not integrate with harm signals (z_harm_a) but with a separate pain-specific pathway, the proposed convergence mechanism needs revision.

mapping.source_claim_statement: The anterior insular cortex re-represents interoceptive afferent signals (from all body systems) as subjective feelings; it is implicated in the full range of emotional and homeostatic conditions and may constitute the neural substrate of awareness via integration of the body's current state.
mapping.ree_translation: AICAnalog receives z_harm_a and drive_level (interoceptive baseline from SD-012) and computes aic_salience. Craig establishes the biological precedent that AIC is precisely the structure that integrates harm-valenced afferents (pain, visceral signals) with homeostatic drive state to generate a salience signal -- exactly the input-output specification of SD-032c.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Craig's paper concerns awareness and subjective phenomenology; it does not explicitly model AIC as a threshold-gated urgency trigger that fires a network-switch signal to a coordinator. That action-switch function is a downstream claim requiring the Menon & Uddin architecture.
mapping.source_context: Comprehensive literature review across imaging, lesion, and single-unit studies in humans and non-human primates.

confidence_components.notes: Nat Rev Neurosci 2009, >5000 citations. Source quality very high. Mapping fidelity limited by Craig focusing on phenomenology rather than urgency-interrupt circuitry.
